Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝无衾苦寒 - 汝 means 'you' (informal); 无衾 means 'without a quilt'; 苦寒 means 'suffering from cold.' This phrase describes the woman's condition of being cold and lacking warmth.
  • 盍就我宿 - 盍 is an archaic particle used to suggest a proposal or invitation; 就我宿 means 'come and stay with me.' This is a polite invitation to share a bed.
  • 大恚詈 - 大恚 means 'greatly angry'; 詈 means 'to curse' or 'to scold.' This describes the woman's strong reaction of anger and cursing.
  • 殊不知恩 - 殊 means 'very' or 'extremely'; 不知恩 means 'ungrateful' or 'not knowing gratitude.' This phrase criticizes the woman for not appreciating the kindness offered.
  • 三都人屡负吾租 - 三都人 refers to people from a specific area (the Three Towns); 屡负吾租 means 'often failed to pay my rent.' This describes the past behavior of the Three Towns people towards the translator.
  • 诮辱我 - 诮 means 'to mock' or 'to ridicule'; 辱 means 'to disgrace.' This describes the past insults and humiliation the translator suffered from the Three Towns people.
  • 真误 - 真 means 'really' or 'truly'; 误 means 'mistaken' or 'wrong.' This phrase expresses the translator's realization of his mistake.
  • 天下固有两三都也 - 固 means 'indeed' or 'certainly'; 有两三都 means 'there are multiple places called Three Towns.' This phrase acknowledges the existence of different places with the same name.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公意怜之 - 公 refers to the translator, who is a public figure or official; 意怜之 means 'had pity on her.' This reflects the translator's compassionate nature.
  • 宰某邑 - 宰 means 'to govern' or 'to be the magistrate'; 某邑 refers to a specific town or county. This indicates the translator's position as a local official.
  • 讼者请罪 - 讼者 means 'the litigant' or 'the person involved in a lawsuit'; 请罪 means 'to plead guilty' or 'to ask for forgiveness.' This describes the litigant's response to the translator's accusation.
  • 沈利梁仪 - 沈利 and 梁仪 are proper nouns that need to be consistently translated.
  • 湖州沈利 - 湖州 is a place name, referring to Huzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province; 沈利 is the name of a person. This provides context for the location and the individual.
  • 故笔贾也 - 故 means 'former'; 笔贾 means 'pen merchant.' This describes the person's previous occupation.
  • 秘制 - 秘制 means 'secret technique' or 'secret method.' This refers to the unique method the person discovered.
  • 选豪极难 - 选豪 means 'selecting the best hair'; 极难 means 'extremely difficult.' This describes the challenge of selecting the finest materials for making pens.
  • 兔鼠诸物 - 兔鼠 refers to rabbits and mice; 诸物 means 'various animals.' This lists the sources of the hair used for making pens.

  • 乃悟冥司之言有以也 - Means 'he then realized that the words of the underworld were true,' indicating his acceptance of fate.
  • 每以述于人，而悔其向之不达 - Means 'he often recounted this to others, regretting his earlier lack of understanding,' showing his reflection on life.
  • 故缢而死 - Means 'and so he died by hanging,' indicating his final act.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏氏 - Refers to the Hui family, a surname mentioned in the text.
  • 黔 - Refers to Guizhou, a region in southern China.
  • 冥司 - Refers to the underworld or the realm of the dead, a common concept in Chinese mythology.
  • 开阁 - A euphemism for suicide by hanging, indicating the old man's final act.
  • 家室怨叹声不绝耳 - Reflects the traditional Chinese family structure and the emotional impact of family misfortune.
  • 诸姬以冻馁求去 - Indicates the social status of concubines and their vulnerability to poverty.
  • 子孙妇之寡者亦相率去帷 - Reflects the traditional role of widows in the family and their tendency to leave after the death of their husbands.

Proper Nouns

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening phrase '湘之始死也，皇皇无所向' introduces a state of aimlessness and disorientation. The phrase '始死' can be ambiguous, as it could mean 'at the beginning of death' or 'in the early stages of death.'
  • The phrase '觉彩霞满天，溪谷绚映如锦绣' uses vivid imagery to describe the surroundings. The use of '觉' (to perceive) adds a sense of immediacy and sensory experience.

  • The phrase '湘张皇失措，莫知所对，满堂粲然' describes the nobleman's panic and the lively atmosphere. The use of '张皇失措' (panicked and disoriented) and '满堂粲然' (the entire hall brightly lit) creates a vivid and dynamic scene.
